summary: Widespread 500 Internal Server Errors on Checkout Workflow
short_description: HikariCP database connection pool exhaustion in booking-service.
description: At 09:15 AM during the holiday promotional flash sale, the customer success team reported that 80% of users were failing to complete reservations. Datadog APM dashboards indicated a massive spike in HTTP 500 errors originating from the 'booking-service' microservice. Application logs showed severe HikariCP timeout exceptions.
work_notes:
[09:18 AM] Checked booking-service pod logs. Found repeating 'org.postgresql.util.PSQLException: FATAL: remaining connection slots are reserved'. 
[09:22 AM] Logged into the primary PostgreSQL node via psql. Ran 'SELECT pid, query, state FROM pg_stat_activity' and found 95 active connections stuck in 'idle in transaction' state for over 3 minutes. 
[09:27 AM] Traced the rogue PIDs back to the new 'billing-orchestrator' deployment. It appears the module opens a transaction block but lacks a 'finally' closure clause if the external tax API times out. 
[09:32 AM] Executed 'SELECT pg_terminate_backend(pid)' on the hanging connections to immediately unblock the database engine. 
[09:35 AM] Edited '/etc/pgbouncer/pgbouncer.ini' to temporarily increase 'max_client_conn' to 5000 to absorb the ongoing flash sale traffic. Reloaded PgBouncer. Connection pool stabilized.
closure_notes: Emergency pool expansion mitigated the immediate dropouts. The root cause was an unhandled exception in the billing module. A hotfix patch (v1.4.2) was deployed at 11:00 AM enforcing strict connection closures. PgBouncer limits reverted to standard.

summary: Premium Suites Showing Unavailable Despite Vacancy
short_description: Stale Redisson distributed locks orphaned in Redis caching layer.
description: Front desk staff escalated an issue where the Presidential and Honeymoon suites were showing as 'Locked for Booking' on the public website, despite no active reservations existing in the central database. The issue persisted for over 2 hours, resulting in lost premium revenue.
work_notes:
[14:10] Verified PostgreSQL 'room_inventory' table. The suites show as status=AVAILABLE. 
[14:15] Pulled logs for the 'inventory-manager' service. Found 'RedisLockException: Could not acquire lock' when trying to sync state. 
[14:20] SSH'd into the redis-prod-cluster. Ran a safe 'SCAN' for keys matching 'lock:room:*'. Found 14 active lock tokens. 
[14:25] Checked the TTL (Time-To-Live) on the keys using the 'TTL' command. They returned '-1', indicating they were set to persist forever. 
[14:30] Correlated the lock timestamps with a Kubernetes node failure that occurred at 11:45 AM. The 'inventory-manager' pods were evicted mid-transaction, failing to release their locks. 
[14:35] Manually executed 'DEL lock:room:PR-101' and 'DEL lock:room:HM-202'. 
[14:40] Called the internal cache flush API endpoint via cURL. Inventory instantly synced.
closure_notes: Orphaned distributed locks were manually evicted from the Redis cluster. Engineering ticket raised to enforce a hard 60-second TTL on all Redisson lock acquisitions to prevent permanent blocking in the event of pod termination.

summary: Zero Payment Conversions and Thread Starvation in Gateway
short_description: Stripe API timeouts causing payment-gateway circuit breaker to trip.
description: Total halt of revenue collection. The payment processing subsystem stopped returning success callbacks. Monitoring showed outbound network traffic to our primary payment provider dropping to zero, while internal queue depth skyrocketed until the service crashed.
work_notes:
[18:05] Triggered Sev-1 incident. The 'payment-gateway' service is throwing 'java.net.SocketTimeoutException: Read timed out to api.stripe.com'. 
[18:12] Checked Resilience4j metrics dashboard. Circuit breaker transitioned from CLOSED to OPEN due to a 100% failure rate over the last 60 seconds. 
[18:15] Verified Stripe status page; they are experiencing a major regional outage in us-east-1. 
[18:20] Initiated manual failover protocol. Accessed the configuration server and updated the global environment variables: set 'GATEWAY_PRIMARY_PROCESSOR' to 'adyen' and 'USE_FALLBACK_PAYMENT' to 'true'. 
[18:25] Sent a SIGHUP command using 'kill -HUP' to the active payment pods to force a hot-reload of the routing configuration without dropping active client connections. 
[18:30] Monitored traffic. Confirmed payloads are successfully routing to Adyen. Conversion rates returning to baseline.
closure_notes: Successfully executed manual fallback to secondary payment processor during third-party outage. System remained on Adyen until Stripe confirmed full resolution at 22:00. Automated failover logic needs to be reviewed as it failed to trigger automatically.

summary: Night Audit Process Failing Due to Recurring Pod Crashes
short_description: Reservation service pods experiencing OOMKilled events during report generation.
description: The automated midnight auditing job, which compiles daily occupancy and financial reports for all hotel properties, failed to complete. The job orchestrator reported that the worker pods were repeatedly crashing and entering a CrashLoopBackOff state.
work_notes:
[01:15 AM] Paged by PagerDuty for night audit failure. 
[01:20 AM] Ran 'kubectl get pods -n production'. Saw 'reservation-service-worker' pods failing with Exit Code 137 (OOMKilled). 
[01:25 AM] Reviewed Kubernetes event logs: 'Memory cgroup out of memory: Killed process'. The pods are hitting their 1Gi hard limit. 
[01:35 AM] Checked the application code commits for the day. A new feature was merged that includes high-resolution images in the generated PDF reports, ballooning the memory footprint of the PDF renderer. 
[01:45 AM] Edited the deployment spec ('kubectl edit deployment'). Increased 'resources.limits.memory' from 1Gi to 2.5Gi. Updated JAVA_TOOL_OPTIONS to '-Xmx2048m' to allow the JVM heap to utilize the new space. 
[01:55 AM] Scaled replicas from 2 to 5 to distribute the backlog of report requests. 
[02:10 AM] Audit completed successfully.
closure_notes: Increased container memory limits and JVM heap sizes to accommodate the new PDF generation requirements. Downscaled the replica count back to baseline after the audit queue cleared. Developers instructed to optimize PDF image compression.

summary: Fluentd Log Shippers Crashing Due to Root Volume Exhaustion
short_description: Application nodes ran out of disk space (/var/log) due to TRACE logging.
description: Multiple backend infrastructure nodes fell out of the active load balancer pool. Health checks failed because the host operating system could no longer write to the root filesystem. Telemetry indicated 100% disk utilization on the /var/log partition.
work_notes:
[10:00] Nodes 03, 04, and 07 reported offline. 
[10:05] SSH access established to Node-04. System threw 'No space left on device' warning upon login. 
[10:10] Executed 'df -h'. Confirmed /dev/sda1 is at 100%. 
[10:15] Ran 'du -sh /var/log/hotel-app/*'. Found a single file, 'booking-engine-trace.log', sitting at 45GB. 
[10:20] Discovered that yesterday's troubleshooting session left the application.properties file with 'logging.level.root=TRACE'. 
[10:25] IMPORTANT: Did NOT run 'rm' as the file is open. Executed 'cat /dev/null > /var/log/hotel-app/booking-engine-trace.log' to truncate the file and instantly reclaim 45GB of blocks without restarting the app. 
[10:35] Reverted the logging level back to INFO via sed string replacement. 
[10:40] Restarted logrotate daemon to ensure future compression. Nodes marked healthy by ALB.
closure_notes: Disk space saturation caused by accidental TRACE level logging combined with a hung logrotate process. Safely truncated the active file to restore service. Added a CI/CD pipeline check to prevent deploying TRACE log configs to production.

summary: Global Authentication Failures and Forced User Logouts
short_description: Desynchronized JWT public keys in booking-service memory cache.
description: Immediately following the scheduled monthly security maintenance window, all active hotel staff and guests were forcefully logged out. Attempts to log back in were successful at the identity gateway, but the booking application rejected the valid tokens with HTTP 401 Unauthorized errors.
work_notes:
[03:00] Maintenance window closed. Key rotation script executed. 
[03:05] Flood of 401 Unauthorized errors detected on the booking API gateway. 
[03:10] Checked booking-service logs: 'SignatureVerificationException: The Token Signature is invalid'. 
[03:15] Realized the booking-service caches the JWKS (JSON Web Key Set) locally for performance. The auth-service rotated the keys, but the booking-service is still using the old public keys to verify the new signatures. 
[03:20] Attempted to hit the internal actuator endpoint to clear the cache: 'curl -X POST /actuator/clearcache/auth-keys'. The request timed out due to network policies blocking direct actuator access from my VPN. 
[03:30] Bypassed the cache clear by forcing a rolling restart of the entire deployment: 'kubectl rollout restart deployment/booking-service'. 
[03:35] Pods initialized and fetched the fresh JWKS keys on startup. Authentication restored.
closure_notes: Security key rotation broke downstream token validation due to aggressive caching. Forced a pod restart to pull new keys. Engineering to implement an automated webhook that notifies downstream services to clear their cache when keys are rotated.

summary: Hotel Search API Returning Empty Results Nationwide
short_description: Elasticsearch cluster state dropped to RED; unassigned primary shards.
description: The primary hotel search bar on the mobile app and website stopped functioning, returning a generic error to users. The backend monitoring systems triggered critical alerts showing the Elasticsearch cluster health was RED, meaning active data was offline.
work_notes:
[11:20] Verified Search API logs. Found 'ClusterHealthIsRedException'. 
[11:25] Forwarded port 9200 and ran 'GET /_cluster/health'. Status is RED. Active primary shards: 98%. 
[11:30] Queried allocation explain: 'GET /_cluster/allocation/explain'. The 'hotel_rooms' index has shard [2] unassigned. The explanation showed the shard was locked because the physical node (es-data-03) experienced a sudden hardware kernel panic and rebooted. 
[11:40] Node es-data-03 is back online but the shard allocation process stalled due to hitting the 'max_retries' limit during the reboot sequence. 
[11:45] Executed manual routing intervention: 'POST /_cluster/reroute?retry_failed=true'. 
[11:50] Watched the cluster state. The shard successfully assigned and began initializing. 
[11:55] Cluster health transitioned to GREEN. Search queries are successfully returning property listings.
closure_notes: A hardware-level reboot of a data node caused a primary shard to stall during reassignment, turning the cluster RED. Forced a manual routing retry. Investigating the AWS EC2 instance kernel panic with infrastructure team.

summary: Severe Latency Loading Customer Reservation Histories
short_description: Sequential table scans on 'bookings' table causing DB CPU pinning.
description: During the morning rush, front desk agents reported that pulling up guest profiles and past reservations was taking upwards of 15 seconds per click. Database telemetry showed the primary PostgreSQL instance CPU utilization pinned at 100%.
work_notes:
[08:45] Latency alerts firing for 'GET /api/v1/customers/{id}/history'. 
[08:50] Logged into Postgres and checked active queries: 'SELECT * FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE state = 'active''. 
[08:55] Identified a massive query queue. All stuck queries are identical: 'SELECT * FROM bookings WHERE customer_id = $1 ORDER BY checkin_date'. 
[09:05] Ran 'EXPLAIN ANALYZE' on the query. The output clearly showed a 'Seq Scan on bookings' with a high cost factor. The table recently crossed 15 million rows, making sequential scans unviable. 
[09:15] Confirmed there is no B-Tree index on 'customer_id' and 'checkin_date'. 
[09:25] Constructed index statement. MUST USE CONCURRENTLY to avoid locking the table during the rush: 'CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_bookings_cust_date ON bookings(customer_id, checkin_date DESC);' 
[09:40] Index creation finished. CPU utilization instantly dropped from 100% to 18%. Query times reduced to 45ms.
closure_notes: Missing relational index caused massive CPU drag via full table scans as the database grew. Applied a concurrent index directly to production to restore performance. Database schema migration scripts updated for future deployments.

summary: Concurrent Booking Attempts Failing with Deadlock Exceptions
short_description: Row-level transactional deadlocks between room_inventory and ledger tables.
description: A specific block of highly discounted promotional rooms triggered a race condition. Hundreds of users attempted to book the same 50 rooms simultaneously. The application began throwing unhandled 500 errors, and the database began logging deadlock terminations.
work_notes:
[15:00] Application error logs spiking with 'org.springframework.dao.DeadlockLoserDataAccessException'. 
[15:10] Examined PostgreSQL logs: 'ERROR: deadlock detected. DETAIL: Process 410 waits for ShareLock on transaction; blocked by process 415'. 
[15:20] Traced the application code path. Discovered that the 'standard_booking' workflow updates the 'room_inventory' table first, then the 'customer_ledger'. However, the 'promo_booking' workflow was coded to update the 'customer_ledger' first, then 'room_inventory'. 
[15:30] This reverse locking order guarantees deadlocks under high concurrency. 
[15:40] Accessed the DB. Ran query against 'pg_locks' to find the top-level blocking PID. 
[15:45] Executed 'pg_terminate_backend()' on the promo-workflow connections to break the deadlock and allow the standard bookings to commit. 
[16:00] Temporarily disabled the promotional code via the admin dashboard to stop the race condition.
closure_notes: Architectural flaw found where different microservice modules modify dependent tables in varying structural orders, causing classic database deadlocks. Promo disabled. Developers must enforce a strict lexicographical sorting order on all row locks.

summary: Automated Invoice Email Delivery Dropping 25% of Payloads
short_description: AWS S3 503 SlowDown errors triggered by flat directory structure throttling.
description: The background worker process responsible for generating PDF invoices and uploading them to AWS S3 prior to emailing the customer began failing. The dead-letter queue filled rapidly. Customers complained they were not receiving checkout receipts.
work_notes:
[12:15] Checked 'invoice-generator' logs. Found thousands of 'AmazonS3Exception: Status Code: 503, Error Code: SlowDown'. 
[12:20] Verified our S3 bucket metrics in AWS CloudWatch. We are hitting 4,000 PUT requests per second, which exceeds the AWS limit of 3,500 PUTs per prefix. 
[12:30] Checked the S3 path logic. All files are being written to 's3://hotel-invoices-prod/2026-06-16/'. Because they share the exact same date prefix, AWS is forcing them onto a single physical storage partition, causing the throttling. 
[12:40] Modified the application config map via Kubernetes. Set 'export INVOICING_USE_RANDOMIZED_PREFIX=true' to prepend a 4-character random alphanumeric hash to the S3 key. 
[12:50] Restarted the invoice deployment to pull the new config. 
[13:00] Paths now look like 's3://hotel-invoices-prod/8a2f-2026-06-16/'. 
[13:10] Monitored CloudWatch. 503 errors dropped to zero as AWS distributed the load across multiple partitions. Cleared the dead-letter queue.
closure_notes: S3 partition limits were exceeded due to a sequential date-based naming convention during high load. Implemented randomized hashing on object prefixes to distribute I/O operations. Backlog of emails successfully dispatched.
